data.title: Origin Materials plans to sell technology, wind down operations
data.description:
Origin Materials (ORGN) down 25.5% post-market Friday after saying its board determined it is in the best interests of its shareholders to sell its PET cap technology and other remaining assets, followed by an orderly wind down of operations.

Origin (ORGN) said its board approved the dissolution and liquidation of the company, subject to shareholder approval, and will call a special shareholder meeting to seek approval of the plan of dissolution; upon filing of the certificate of dissolution, it intends to cease trading in its common stock and warrants.

As part of an imminent workforce reduction, members of the executive team including CEO John Bissell will leave the company, with CFO and COO Matt Plavan appointed as interim CEO.

The company said it will provide an estimate of any amount that may be distributed to shareholders, but the amount actually distributable "may vary substantially from any estimate provided by the company, based on a number of factors."

data.title: Both up 7%: RIOT Platform sets earnings tone—will MSTR follow? Full story
data.description:
Bitcoin-linked stocks RIOT Platforms (RIOT) and Strategy (MSTR) rallied nearly 7% on May 1 as Bitcoin (BTC-USD) climbed back towards the $77K level. 

But the bigger question here is—did Riot Platform's earnings just set the tone for what Strategy could deliver next?

Riot Platforms reported Q1 revenue of $167.2M, up 3.6% Y/Y and beating analyst estimates by nearly $37.2M. However, GAAP EPS came in at -$1.44 vs. -$0.83 expected, hurt by a $326.7M non-cash Bitcoin revaluation loss and $97.7M depreciation.

Even though operating strength stood out. CFO Jamie Chung highlighted $21M in power credits, helping lower its Bitcoin mining cost to $44.6K per asset.

The company mined 1,473 Bitcoins in Q1, down 3.7% Y/Y, while holdings fell 18% to 15,680 BTC. Still, the company's average operating hash rate rose 23% to 36.4 EH/s.

A key upside was diversification—Riot Platform's data center revenue hit $33.2M in Q1, while Advanced Micro Devices doubled its capacity at its Rockdale site to 50 MW, strengthening its AI story.

Analyst sentiment also remains strong. Seeking Alpha analysts rate Riot Platforms a “Buy” with a 4.00 score, while Wall Street analysts maintain a stronger 4.57 “Strong Buy” rating.

This optimism also lifted Strategy ahead of its May 5 Q1 earnings report. Analysts expect the firm to report Q1 EPS of -18.98 on revenue of $125.1M. 

However, estimate revisions remain weak, with 0 EPS upgrades vs. 2 downgrades and 3 revenue upgrades vs. 1 downgrade.

Wall Street still keeps faith in the story, with a strong buy rating of 4.73, but the setup is simple: Riot Platforms already showed how crypto stocks react to Bitcoin strength—now the market is waiting to see if Strategy can follow the same path or break from it.

data.title: Moderna expects up to 10% 2026 revenue growth and $4.5B-$5B year-end cash, while targeting ~$4.2B cash costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moderna (MRNA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Moderna described Q1 performance as driven by international execution, with CEO Stéphane Bancel saying, "In the first quarter, we grew year-over-year revenues significantly to $0.4 billion, driven primarily by execution of our long-term strategic partnership in -- with the U.K. government" and adding, "With a strong Q1, we are reiterating up to 10% growth in 2026."
  • Bancel highlighted new EU regulatory milestones and their timing, stating, "we achieved an important milestone with the approval of mCOMBRIAX in the European Union" and "We also secured approval for mNEXSPIKE in the European Union," adding, "These 2 new approved products in Europe will be important growth drivers in the EU in 2027."
  • CFO James Mock emphasized Q1 revenue mix and near-term revenue expectations, saying, "For the first quarter, total revenue was $400 million" and "For the second quarter, we are expecting revenue of between $50 million and $100 million."
  • Mock detailed the Arbutus/Genevant settlement mechanics and uncertainty on the additional payment, stating, "we will make a lump sum payment of $950 million in the third quarter of 2026" and "If, however, the Federal Circuit affirms liability under Section 1498, Moderna has agreed to make an additional payment of up to $1.3 billion."
  • President Stephen Hoge framed the multi-year plan around partnerships and 2027 EU opportunity, saying, "we expect up to 10% revenue growth driven by our long-term strategic partnerships in the United Kingdom, Canada and Australia, and supported by the continued growth of mNEXSPIKE" and describing Europe as "the large $1.8 billion annual European respiratory vaccines market."

Outlook

  • Mock reiterated the 2026 framework and key assumptions, stating, "we expect total revenue to grow up to 10% in 2026" and "this guidance assumes no revenue from our flu vaccine or mCOMBRIAX."
  • Mock updated cost of sales guidance due to litigation, saying, "Our cost of sales projection has increased from $0.9 billion to $1.8 billion, and now includes the $0.9 billion litigation settlement charge."
  • Mock maintained cost targets and year-end liquidity outlook, stating, "we are expecting total GAAP operating expenses of $4.9 billion and cash costs of $4.2 billion" and "we expect to end 2026 with between $4.5 billion to $5 billion of cash and investments."
  • Compared with the prior call’s U.S. flu posture, management’s language shifted from Q4’s "disappointment" around the FDA process to Q1’s U.S. timing clarity, with Bancel saying, "In the U.S., our seasonal flu vaccine mRNA-1010 was assigned a PDUFA date of August 5."

Financial results

  • Mock reported Q1 revenue and a cost-of-sales impact tied to the settlement, stating, "revenue was $400 million in the quarter" and "Cost of sales for the quarter was $955 million. This includes $878 million related to our previously disclosed litigation settlement."
  • Mock described operating expense reductions, saying, "R&D expenses for the quarter were $649 million" and "SG&A expenses for the quarter were $173 million."
  • Mock quantified GAAP and non-GAAP loss views and liquidity, stating, "Net loss for the quarter was $1.3 billion or $3.40 per share" and "Excluding this item, the net loss would have been $0.5 billion or $1.18 per share" and "We ended the first quarter with cash and investments of $7.5 billion."

Q&A

  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "you newly disclosed initiation of a Phase III study for Intismeran as monotherapy"; President Hoge: "we're looking at, therefore, INT as monotherapy as opposed to just surgery and watchful waiting for high-risk Stage I disease."
  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "is it possible this is a double order year?"; President Hoge: "a second campaign is planned for the fall, and so the third and fourth quarter of this year."
  • Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: "can you refine that at all" on melanoma interim timing; President Hoge: "we won't refine that guidance" and "There is not a built-in futility assessment."
  • Luca Issi, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: "walk us through why the legal team deemed the additional $1.3 billion charge on 1498 is not probable"; CFO Mock: "we're not really going to comment too much on the merits of the trial" and "perhaps late 2027, maybe into 2028 is where we think that this might be resolved."
  • Tyler Van Buren, TD Cowen, Research Division: "what constitutes success" in adjuvant melanoma; President Hoge: "we think the opportunity for benefit could be anywhere between that 0.5 that we saw in a number like 0.8."
  • Eliana Merle, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: "what your expectations are for timing for data from RCC and muscle invasive bladder cancer"; President Hoge: "it's hard to guide right now" and "we can't guide on when that timing would be."
  • Michael Yee, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked whether interim implies hazard ratio differences and RCC timing; President Hoge: "we definitely have reserved alpha for both of those" and "The DSMB will look at that result and then advise us."
  • Courtney Breen, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division: asked on INT revenue recognition and Stage I market; CFO Mock: "it will end up being that we deliver the product to Merck"; President Hoge: "almost 1/3, north of 30% of diagnoses are now earlier stage."
  • Geoffrey Meacham, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked other IO combinations and norovirus bar; President Hoge: "most of our focus is on the combination with the PD-1 and KEYTRUDA" and "we want to see a vaccine efficacy that's also meaningful and so north of 50%."
  • Cory Kasimov, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked OS necessity in melanoma; President Hoge: "RFS is a pretty good predictor."
  • Simon Baker, Rothschild & Co Redburn, Research Division: asked Q2 mix and phasing; CFO Mock: "This was largely, the majority is COVID still" and "Last year, we had $700 million of sales outside the United States."
  • Lin Tsai, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked BD beyond mRNA; CEO Bancel: "If we find the right opportunity to increase what we can do, we will, of course, execute" and "we don't have a pipeline problem like most companies in the industry."
  • Alexandria Hammond, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked EU combo commercialization; President Hoge: "we do not expect revenues in the year of approval" and "We do hope for a successful launch in '27."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-skeptical, pressing on regulatory/legal uncertainty and timing, including "why the legal team deemed the additional $1.3 billion charge on 1498 is not probable" (Luca Issi, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division) and repeated requests to narrow timing like "can you refine that at all" (Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley, Research Division).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded on specifics in Q&A, with President Hoge saying, "we won't refine that guidance" and CFO Mock saying, "we're not really going to comment too much on the merits of the trial."
  • Versus Q4, the flu-related tone moved from uncertainty to process clarity, shifting from Q4’s "disappointment" framing to Q1’s "PDUFA date of August 5" (CEO Bancel) and "we would describe that as a pretty normal course" (President Hoge).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest change from Q4 was the U.S. flu regulatory status moving from "FDA Refusal-to-File" (Q4, CEO Bancel) to "assigned a PDUFA date of August 5" (Q1, CEO Bancel), alongside EU approvals for mCOMBRIAX and mNEXSPIKE becoming concrete Q1 milestones.
  • Financial framing shifted to incorporate the Arbutus/Genevant settlement into 2026 guidance, with CFO Mock updating, "Our cost of sales projection has increased from $0.9 billion to $1.8 billion."
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s emphasis on flu RTF implications toward Q1 concentration on INT trial design/interims and the litigation timeline, including "late 2027, maybe into 2028" (CFO Mock).

Risks and concerns

  • Litigation remains a key uncertainty, with CFO Mock stating, "If... the Federal Circuit affirms liability under Section 1498, Moderna has agreed to make an additional payment of up to $1.3 billion" and adding, "We have concluded that a loss related to this pending Section 1498 proceeding is not probable."
  • COVID demand pressure remains embedded in guidance assumptions, with Mock saying, "Our 2026 revenue guidance factors in potential future declines in COVID vaccination rates."
  • Regulatory timing and market access execution in Europe were framed as gating items for combo uptake, with President Hoge saying market access is "pricing and reimbursement" and noting that in Europe "that takes a couple of years as a process."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 as an internationally driven revenue quarter anchored by U.K. partnership deliveries, reiterated "up to 10%" 2026 revenue growth and a ~$4.2B adjusted cash cost objective, and positioned EU approvals of mCOMBRIAX and mNEXSPIKE as 2027 growth drivers. The call also centered investor attention on the Arbutus/Genevant settlement’s cash timing and the Section 1498 appeal path, while Q&A concentrated on the expanding Intismeran Phase III program design and event-driven readout timing across oncology and norovirus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BlackRock Flexible Income ETF declares monthly distribution of $0.2201
data.description:
• BlackRock Flexible Income ETF (BINC) - $0.2201.
  • Payable May 06; for shareholders of record May 01; ex-div May 01.

data.title: Vanda rises after hours on launch of tradipitant for motion sickness
data.description:
• Vanda Pharmaceuticals (VNDA) is up ~3% in after-hours trading Friday after announcing the availability of Nereus (tradipitant) for the prevention of vomiting induced by motion in adults.
  • The company said that Nereus is the first new drug treatment for motion sickness in more than four decades.
  • Nereus is an oral neurokinin-1 (NK-1) receptor antagonist.
  • Vanda said through a website, cash pay patients could get the drug for $85 per capsule.

data.title: GameStop preparing offer for eBay - WSJ
data.description:
GameStop (GME) is preparing to make an offer for eBay (EBAY), reported The Wall Street Journal on Friday.

According to the article, citing people familiar with the matter, GameStop has been quietly building a stake in eBay’s shares ahead of a potential offer.

eBay, which has a market cap of over $46B, rose 10.2% in extended trading.

GameStop could submit an offer for eBay as soon as later this month, WSJ said. 

If eBay isn’t receptive, GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen could decide to take the offer directly to eBay’s shareholders, the report added, citing one of the people.

GameStop and eBay did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Advanced Gold Exploration names Jason Baker as new CFO
data.description:
• Advanced Gold Exploration announced on Friday that, further to his role as director of the company, Jason Baker has been appointed CFO, replacing David MacDonald.
  • Jason Baker is a finance professional at Altus Capital Partners and has helped raise over $35M in equity financings over the past two years for companies including Scorpio Gold (SRCRF) and Magma Silver (MGMA:CA).
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Huntsman outlines $5-plus million 2026 contribution from semiconductor-grade specialty amines as it pushes price to stay ahead of raw materials
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huntsman (HUN) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Huntsman’s near-term narrative centered on pricing discipline and stronger order patterns tied to the Middle East conflict’s cost and logistics impact. Chairman, President & CEO Peter Huntsman said, "From the first hours of this conflict, our #1 commercial priority has been to increase prices enough to offset rising costs. I believe we've been successful in doing this." He added that operations execution has been strong: "Our operations during the first quarter and going into the second quarter have been excellent."

  • Management described demand in early Q2 as better than expected, while emphasizing limited forward visibility. CEO Huntsman said, "From a sales perspective, we are seeing stronger-than-expected demand going well into the second quarter," and attributed it to "seasonality," "customers who are buying ahead of the expected price increases," and "disruptions that have been seen in certain trade flows." He cautioned, "I can't see order patterns that go through the month of June," and later added, "Today, that visibility is less clear as we look further into the quarter."

  • In prepared remarks and Q&A, management framed a margin-recovery push after a prolonged downturn. CEO Huntsman said, "We are aggressively raising our prices to both cover our cost of our raw materials while also expanding margins from the trough economics that we've been experiencing for the past 3 years," while acknowledging, "we still have a ways to go to get to our normalized margin levels."

  • CFO commentary during Q&A emphasized where economics are improving in China and how price capture is expected to work. Executive VP & CFO Philip Lister said, "where the Chinese joint venture is making money today is on propylene oxide and the margins that we're seeing there over and above propylene," and added on the step-up dynamics, "we are seeing pricing exceed the raw material increases."

Outlook

  • Management’s Q2 setup highlighted raw-material inflation and intended price recovery. CEO Huntsman said, "We're projecting in the second quarter that we'll take in well in excess of around $100 million of raw material costs, and we expect to offset that and get prices higher than that," while stressing that "demand" and "raw material stability" are needed to lift polyurethane profitability.

  • On Europe’s trajectory, management stated improving conditions, with a clear EBITDA expectation as of the call date. CFO Lister said, "as we sit here today, we would expect Europe to be positive from an EBITDA perspective."

  • Management declined to provide Q3 specifics and repeatedly tied the second half to demand elasticity and macro conditions. CEO Huntsman said, "far too early to comment on Q3," and later added, "Q3 is going to be more demand-driven than anything else."

Financial results

  • The transcript did not include consolidated revenue, EPS, or companywide adjusted EBITDA figures; management instead discussed segment EBITDA ranges and operational drivers. CEO Huntsman addressed Performance Products guidance by referencing a prior-quarter baseline: "in your guide, you're going from $26 million in EBITDA to an estimate of $30 million to $40 million in the second quarter."

  • Management quantified potential impact from the Saudi ethyleneamines joint venture’s logistics disruption. CEO Huntsman said the facility is "operating today" at "around 50%" and estimated, "that impact could be as high as $4 million or $5 million for the quarter," adding variability due to higher-cost trucking.

  • Specialty semiconductor-grade amines ramp remained a 2026 earnings item with qualification timing constraints. CEO Huntsman said, "It's going through qualifications" that are "usually around 9 to 12 months," and projected, "by the end of the year, we'll probably see $5-plus million coming from that this year."

Q&A

  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: asked about supply-side rationalization for MDI/polyurethanes; Chairman, President & CEO Peter Huntsman said, "I don't see a great deal of structural change as we look at MDI," while noting Europe energy-cost pressure and potential shifts in Chinese export economics.

  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: asked on Advanced Materials durability (aerospace/power); Chairman, President & CEO Huntsman said, "the performance we're seeing in Advanced Materials is largely what we expected a quarter ago" and reiterated recovery in "aerospace, power."

  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research: asked about MDI operating rates and post-war change; Chairman, President & CEO Huntsman said industry is "probably looking at the low to mid-80s" while Huntsman is "in the high 80s" and "sold out completely" in China.

  • Kevin McCarthy, Vertical Research: asked about PO/MTBE JV earnings; CEO Huntsman said it is "in the low to mid-single-digit millions of dollars of impact," and CFO Lister clarified, "MTBE margins aren't what you would expect in China."

  • Frank Mitsch, Fermium: asked about MDI pricing vs benzene; CEO Huntsman said, "we're certainly staying ahead of the benzene curve" and expects "margin expansion."

  • Hassan Ahmed, Alembic: asked about MDI outages/feedstocks and timing; CEO Huntsman referenced a Middle East producer at "roughly about 4% of global capacity" and said demand sustainability into H2 is the key unknown.

  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: asked why Performance Products isn’t stronger given maleic; CEO Huntsman said maleic strength should be "manifest through Q2 going into Q3" but cited a Strait-related headwind at the Saudi JV.

  • Mike Harrison, Seaport: asked for mid-cycle polyurethane margins; CEO Huntsman said, "that business on average ought to be a mid-teen sort of a business."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, focusing on sustainability, spreads, and Europe profitability; questions repeatedly probed durability ("how long does it continue?" themes), with direct pressure on segment underperformance and guide ranges.

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive but cautious, pairing confidence on near-term demand/pricing with macro uncertainty; CEO Huntsman said, "I'm glad to see the trends" but added, "my only question is how sustainable is it?"

  • Versus the prior quarter, the discussion shifted from “green shoots” and recovery hope to conflict-driven price action and supply-chain dislocation; management’s caution on visibility appeared higher this quarter ("that visibility is less clear"), while analysts leaned more into timing, elasticity, and disruption impacts.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The prior call emphasized cost savings and broader strategic optionality; CFO Lister previously said, "We targeted $100 million of cost savings overall" and guided "about $45 million of in-year savings" for 2026, while the current call emphasized price increases to offset raw inflation and capture margin.

  • The current quarter’s analyst focus concentrated on MDI utilization, benzene pass-through, pre-buying, and Strait-linked disruptions; the prior quarter’s questions included leverage/covenants and consolidation/M&A framing.

  • Management’s language moved from hopeful recovery to more tactical execution under volatility; CEO Huntsman previously said, "we remain hopeful" about early Europe signs, while now said, "I struggle to see how inflationary pressures... will not see an inevitable downward pressure later in the year."

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged demand sustainability risk as price increases and inflation filter through end markets. CEO Huntsman said, "I struggle to see how inflationary pressures... will not see an inevitable downward pressure later in the year," and repeatedly tied H2 outcomes to demand.

  • Supply-chain/logistics disruptions were an explicit operational risk, particularly around the Strait of Hormuz. CEO Huntsman said that if an agreement were reached, resuming full export by sea would take "30 to 45 days."

  • Europe competitiveness remained a structural concern tied to energy pricing and policy; CEO Huntsman said, "I continue to be concerned" about the U.K. aniline facility’s economics due to "really poor energy policies."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 and early Q2 as a period of stronger-than-expected demand, higher industry utilization, and aggressive pricing intended to offset significant raw-material inflation while rebuilding margins after a multi-year trough. At the same time, leadership repeatedly framed the second half as demand-dependent, highlighting limited visibility beyond June, inflation-driven elasticity risk, and logistics uncertainty tied to the Strait of Hormuz, while pointing to incremental 2026 contributions from specialty amines tied to semiconductors and continued momentum in Advanced Materials end markets like aerospace and power.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: KB Home CFO Robert Dillard resigns
data.description:
• KB Home (KBH) said Robert Dillard, the company's Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, resigned from his position, effective May 8, 2026.
  • Dillard’s decision was not related to any disagreement with KB Home or its financial or accounting policies or practices.
  • Filing

data.title: Fulton Financial prices $300M subordinated notes offering
data.description:
• Fulton Financial (FULT) priced a $300M offering of fixed-to-floating rate subordinated notes due 2036.
  • The notes carry a 5.95% fixed interest rate through May 2031, then switch to a floating rate tied to SOFR plus 217 basis points.
  • Interest will be paid semi-annually during the fixed period and quarterly during the floating period.
  • The notes are callable at par starting May 15, 2031.
  • Proceeds will be used to repay $195 million of existing 3.25% subordinated notes due 2030 and for general corporate purposes.
  • The offering is expected to close around May 5, 2026.
